exams.name: GRE General Test
exams.description: The GRE General Test measures verbal reasoning, quantitative reasoning, critical thinking, and analytical writing skills for graduate and business school admissions.

exams.name: SAT
exams.description: The SAT is a standardized test widely used for college admissions in the United States, assessing evidence-based reading and writing as well as math skills.

exams.name: ACT
exams.description: The ACT is a college admissions test that evaluates English, math, reading, and science reasoning skills, with an optional writing section.

exams.name: GMAT
exams.description: The GMAT is a standardized exam used for business school admissions, evaluating analytical writing, integrated reasoning, quantitative, and verbal skills.

exams.name: TOEFL iBT
exams.description: The TOEFL iBT exam measures the ability of non-native English speakers to use and understand English at the university level across reading, listening, speaking, and writing.

exams.name: IELTS Academic
exams.description: The IELTS Academic test assesses English language proficiency for higher education and immigration through listening, reading, writing, and speaking sections.

questions.text: If 3x - 5 = 16, what is the value of x?
A price is increased by 20% and then discounted by 20%. What is the net percent change in price?
questions.explanation: Starting from 100, a 20% increase gives 120. A 20% discount on 120 gives 96. Net change: 4% decrease.

questions.text: Which of the following is the solution set of the inequality 2x + 3 < 7?
A rectangular field has a perimeter of 120 meters and a length that is twice its width. What is the area of the field?
questions.explanation: Let width = w, length = 2w. Perimeter = 2(l + w) = 2(3w) = 6w = 120 ⇒ w = 20, l = 40. Area = 20 × 40 = 800, so choice A. (Wait, check options: correct is 800 m².)

questions.text: A data set has mean 10 and standard deviation 2. Which of the following values is 1.5 standard deviations above the mean?
questions.explanation: 1.5 standard deviations above mean: 10 + 1.5×2 = 10 + 3 = 13.

questions.text: Column A: (3/5)^2  Column B: 3/25. Which is greater?

choices.text: The relationship cannot be determined from the information given.

questions.explanation: (3/5)^2 = 9/25, which is greater than 3/25. So Column A > Column B.

questions.text: Text Completion: The scientist’s theory, though initially dismissed as ______, was eventually recognized as a groundbreaking insight.
questions.explanation: The contrast between "dismissed" and "groundbreaking" suggests the theory was seen as heretical or unacceptable at first.

questions.text: Sentence Equivalence: Far from being ______, the committee’s decision was the result of months of careful deliberation.
questions.explanation: We need two similar words meaning "not carefully thought out"; impulsive, capricious, and hasty fit, but the best pair indicating rashness is impulsive and hasty.

questions.text: Reading Comprehension: The passage suggests that one limitation of the study was its reliance on self-reported data because such data are often:
questions.explanation: Self-reported data are commonly criticized for being biased, reflecting participants’ perceptions rather than objective facts.

questions.text: Select the word that is most nearly opposite in meaning to: "proliferate".
questions.explanation: Proliferate means to increase or multiply; its opposite is to shrink or dwindle.

questions.text: If 4x + 2 = 3x + 10, what is the value of x?
questions.explanation: 4x + 2 = 3x + 10 ⇒ x = 8? Actually: 4x - 3x = 10 - 2 ⇒ x = 8. So correct answer is 8.

questions.text: The graph of y = 2x + b passes through the point (1, 5). What is the value of b?
questions.explanation: Substitute (1,5): 5 = 2(1) + b ⇒ b = 3.

questions.text: A right triangle has legs of length 6 and 8. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
questions.explanation: By the Pythagorean theorem: √(6² + 8²) = √(36 + 64) = √100 = 10.

questions.text: In the passage, the author’s primary purpose is to:

choices.text: argue for a change in policy

questions.explanation: The passage emphasizes the need to revise existing regulations, indicating the author’s purpose is to argue for a change in policy.

questions.text: As used in line 23, the word "charged" most nearly means:
questions.explanation: In context, "charged" is used figuratively to mean full of or filled with emotion.

questions.text: Choose the best version of the underlined portion: "The committee have decided to postpone its decision."

choices.text: have decided to postpone its decision.
has decided to postpone their decision.
has decided to postpone its decision.
have decided to postpone their decision.

questions.explanation: "Committee" is a collective noun treated as singular here, so use "has" and "its".

questions.text: Choose the answer that best completes the sentence: "The students packed their bags, ______ the bus arrived moments later."
questions.explanation: The two independent clauses occur in sequence with no contrast or cause; "and" is most appropriate.

questions.text: Which choice most effectively combines the two sentences? "The museum expanded in 2015. It added two new exhibition halls."

choices.text: The museum expanded in 2015, and it added two new exhibition halls.
The museum, expanding in 2015, it added two new exhibition halls.
In 2015, the museum expanded, adding two new exhibition halls.
The museum expanded, it added two new exhibition halls in 2015.

questions.explanation: Choice C combines the sentences concisely and correctly, avoiding comma splices and redundancy.

questions.text: Which revision corrects the error in modifier placement? "Walking down the street, the skyscrapers towered above Maria."

choices.text: Walking down the street, the skyscrapers towered above her.
Walking down the street, Maria saw the skyscrapers towering above her.
The skyscrapers, walking down the street, towered above Maria.
The skyscrapers towered above Maria walking down the street.

questions.explanation: The modifier "Walking down the street" must clearly describe Maria, not the skyscrapers. Choice B fixes the dangling modifier.

questions.text: According to the passage, why did the researchers choose coral reefs as their primary study sites?

choices.text: Coral reefs are easier to access than other marine ecosystems.
Coral reefs are highly sensitive indicators of environmental change.
Coral reefs are less diverse than previously thought.
Coral reefs have remained unchanged for millions of years.

questions.explanation: The passage states that coral reefs respond quickly to shifts in water temperature and chemistry, making them good indicators of environmental change.

flashcards.backText: showing great attention to detail; very careful and precise
flashcards.exampleSentence: The researcher kept meticulous records of every stage of the experiment.

flashcards.backText: seeming reasonable or probable
flashcards.exampleSentence: The scientist offered a plausible explanation for the unexpected results.

flashcards.backText: to make suffering, deficiency, or a problem less severe
flashcards.exampleSentence: New public transport routes may help alleviate traffic congestion in the city.

flashcards.backText: possible to do easily or conveniently
flashcards.exampleSentence: The engineers discussed whether it was feasible to complete the project within six months.

flashcards.backText: preventing success or development; harmful; unfavorable
flashcards.exampleSentence: The report highlights the adverse effects of pollution on marine life.

flashcards.backText: certain to happen; unavoidable
flashcards.exampleSentence: With so many factories nearby, some level of noise was inevitable.

flashcards.backText: to make less severe, serious, or painful
flashcards.exampleSentence: Planting trees can help mitigate the impact of air pollution.

flashcards.backText: a lack of compatibility or similarity between two or more facts
flashcards.exampleSentence: The accountant noticed a discrepancy between the reported income and the bank statements.

flashcards.backText: widespread in a particular area or at a particular time
flashcards.exampleSentence: Obesity is becoming increasingly prevalent among children in urban areas.

flashcards.backText: a belief or idea
flashcards.exampleSentence: Many people still have the outdated notion that learning a new language is only for young students.

flashcards.backText: able to be maintained at a certain rate or level without causing damage to the environment or resources
flashcards.exampleSentence: The government is investing in sustainable energy sources such as wind and solar power.

flashcards.backText: logical and consistent; forming a unified whole
flashcards.exampleSentence: The candidate delivered a coherent argument supported by strong evidence.

flashcards.backText: open to more than one interpretation; ambiguous
flashcards.exampleSentence: The CEO gave an equivocal answer when asked about future layoffs.

flashcards.backText: wastefully extravagant; lavishly or recklessly spending
flashcards.exampleSentence: His prodigal habits quickly used up the inheritance.

flashcards.backText: expressed clearly; easy to understand
flashcards.exampleSentence: The professor gave a lucid explanation of the complex theory.

flashcards.backText: showing a willingness to take surprisingly bold risks; also, showing an impudent lack of respect
flashcards.exampleSentence: Their audacious plan to disrupt the industry attracted many investors.

flashcards.backText: well meaning and kindly
flashcards.exampleSentence: The scholarship was funded by a benevolent organization.

flashcards.backText: existing or occurring at or on the surface; not thorough, deep, or complete
flashcards.exampleSentence: Her analysis of the novel was too superficial to earn a high score.

flashcards.backText: involving many carefully arranged parts or details; detailed and complicated
flashcards.exampleSentence: The architect presented an elaborate proposal for the new library.

flashcards.backText: to accept or allow behavior that is considered morally wrong or offensive to continue
flashcards.exampleSentence: The teacher refused to condone cheating on the exam.

course_modules.title: Geometry, Data Analysis, and Practice Tests

lessons.title: Introduction to SAT Math & Calculator Policies
lessons.description: Overview of the SAT Math section structure, scoring, and permitted calculator use.

lessons.title: Integers, Factors, and Multiples
lessons.description: Review of integer properties, factoring, and common multiples for SAT-style problems.

lessons.title: Fractions, Decimals, and Percents
lessons.description: Converting between forms and solving ratio and percent questions.

lessons.title: Exponents and Roots Basics
lessons.description: Rules of exponents, square roots, and simplifying expressions.

lessons.title: Order of Operations and Algebraic Expressions
lessons.description: Applying PEMDAS and simplifying expressions with variables.

lessons.title: Solving One-Variable Linear Equations
lessons.description: Step-by-step strategies for solving linear equations and checking solutions.

lessons.title: Linear Inequalities and Interval Notation
lessons.description: Graphing inequalities on the number line and interpreting solution sets.

lessons.title: Systems of Linear Equations
lessons.description: Solving systems by substitution and elimination, with word problem examples.

lessons.title: Slope, Intercept, and Graphing Lines
lessons.description: Understanding slope-intercept form and interpreting graphs in context.

lessons.title: Linear Word Problems and Rates of Change
lessons.description: Translating real-world scenarios into linear equations and interpreting slope.

lessons.title: Ratios, Proportions, and Scale
lessons.description: Solving ratio and proportion questions, including maps and scale drawings.

lessons.title: Percent Change and Compound Percent
lessons.description: Handling percent increase/decrease and multi-step percent problems.

lessons.title: Functions: Notation and Evaluation
lessons.description: Reading function notation, evaluating functions, and interpreting tables and graphs.

lessons.title: Linear Functions and Graph Features
lessons.description: Identifying intercepts, zeros, and intervals where functions increase or decrease.

lessons.title: Nonlinear Relationships: Quadratic and Exponential Basics
lessons.description: Recognizing quadratic and exponential growth patterns and key features of their graphs.

lessons.title: Angles, Triangles, and Polygons
lessons.description: Angle relationships, triangle properties, and polygon sums on the SAT.

lessons.title: Circles, Area, and Volume
lessons.description: Applying circle formulas and volume of prisms, cylinders, and pyramids.

lessons.title: Data Interpretation: Tables, Charts, and Graphs
lessons.description: Reading and interpreting data displays, including multi-step questions.

lessons.title: Statistics: Mean, Median, and Standard Deviation
lessons.description: Conceptual understanding of basic statistics and SAT-style applications.

lessons.title: Timed Mixed Practice Set & Review
lessons.description: A full mixed set of SAT Math practice questions with walkthrough explanations.
